svg.text: Greedy Proof — Why “Merge Two Smallest” Is Optimal
In any optimal prefix-code tree, the two lowest-frequency characters a, b can be siblings at
Proof sketch: take any optimal tree T. Let x, y be two siblings at max depth. Swap a ↔ x and
b ↔ y; cost change = (freq(x) − freq(a)) · (depth(a) − depth(x)) + similar for b, y. Both
terms are ≤ 0 since a, b are the smallest. New tree is ≤ cost, therefore optimal.
Let C be the alphabet. After merging a, b into a super-char z with freq(z) = freq(a) +
freq(b), we have alphabet C’ = C − {a, b} ∪ {z} of size n − 1.
• Given optimal T’ for C’, replace leaf z by an internal node with children a, b →
tree for C with cost T’.cost + freq(a) + freq(b).
• Given optimal T for C with a, b as deepest siblings (by lemma), collapsing them to z
gives a tree for C’ with cost T.cost − freq(a) − freq(b).
By induction on alphabet size, Huffman’s algorithm produces an optimal prefix code.
